english: of the rainbow Pth.; ʿja-tson yal-ba ('ja' tshon yal ba) the vanishing of the rainbow frq.; ʿja-lus ('ja' lus) v. lus (lus).
1. also ʿja-mo ('ja' mo) Sch. lame, gen. ža-ba (zha ba); ʿja-bar byed-pa ('ja' bar byed pa) to make lame, to lame S.g. — 2. to bespeak, to concert, to confederate Sch.
ʿja-mo ('ja' mo), edict, diploma, a permit Cs., who declares this word to be Chinese.
Lex. w.e., acc. to Cs. = ʿbyar-ba ('byar ba) to stick together, to cohere.

pf. bčal (bcal), fut. gžal (gzhal), imp. ʿjol ('jol), W. *čal-čé* (*cal ce*), 1. to weigh, ʿjal-byed sraṅ ('jal byed srang) (a pair of) scales for weighing Lex., sraṅ-la gžal-ba (srang la gzhal ba) Glr. — 2. to measure, riṅ-tuṅ-tsád ʿjal-ba (ring thung tshad 'jal ba) C. to measure the length. — 3. to appraise, to tax; to weigh in one's mind, to ponder; more fully expressed by blos-ʿjal-ba (blos 'jal ba) to understand Sch., although native grammarians refer this signification with less probability to mjal-ba (mjal ba). — 4. to pay, pay back, repay, bú-lon (bu lon) a debt, skyin-pa (skyin pa) a loan, k̇ral (khral) a tax; to retaliate, return, repay, esp. with lan (lan): ṗan-lán gnód-pas (phan lan gnod pas) or légs-pai lan nyés-pas (legs pa'i lan nyes pas) to return evil for good. The following is a Buddhist principle of law, but prob. existing only in theory: dkon-mčóg-gi rdzás-la k̇ri ʿjal (dkon mchog gi rdzas la khri 'jal), dge-ʿdún-gyi rdzás-la brgyád-ču ʿjal (dge 'dun gyi rdzas la brgyad cu 'jal), ṗál-pai rdzás-la bdún-gyur ṅo brgyad ʿjal (phal pa'i rdzas la bdun gyur ngo brgyad 'jal) divine or sacred objects are to be repaid or made good tenthousandfold, things or property of the clergy eightyfold, of ordinary men sevenfold, and besides the object itself, hence eightfold C.; in Glr. there is the following passage: brkús-pa la brgyad ʿjal ṅos daṅ dgu (brkus pa la brgyad 'jal ngos dang dgu). — 5. often erron. for mjál-ba (mjal ba); thus prob. also in: ʿjal ʿbúl-ba ('jal 'bul ba) to bring a present Sch. (more correctly: a present of salutation). — ʿjál-k̇a ('jal kha) the act, or business of measuring C.
1. Cs., also lji-ba (lji ba), a flea. — 2. Lex. and Cs.: = ʿjim-pa ('jim pa). — 3. Cs. = ʿjám-pa ('jam pa) soft, smooth. — 4. Sch.: disgusting, nasty, e.g. of a fishy smell.

(receptacle of all that is perishable) 1. the external world: a. acc. to the common (popular) notion: the whole earth, the universe, ʿjig-rtén-na dkón-pa ('jig rten na dkon pa), what is rare, the only thing of its kind in the world Dzl.; ʿjig-rtén-gyi lha ('jig rten gyi lha) the god of the world, a deity of the Bonpos Mil.; ʿjig-rtén-las ʿdás-pa ('jig rten las 'das pa) one that has escaped from this world, one emancipated, blessed Cs. — b. the external world acc. to Brahmanic and Buddhist theories, as set forth: Köpp. I, 231; ʿjig-rtén-gyi k̇ams ('jig rten gyi khams) id. Glr.; ʿjig-rtén čágs-pa ('jig rten chags pa) origin, beginning, gnás-pa (gnas pa) duration, ʿjig-pa ('jig pa) destruction, bžág-pa (bzhag pa) arrangement of the world, cosmography (title of a volume of Stg.) ʿjig-rtén gsum ('jig rten gsum) the three worlds, earth, heaven, and hades; ʿjig-rtén (gsum-gyi) mgón-po ('jig rten gsum gyi mgon po) (Triloknāth Hind.) lord or patron of the three worlds, which is also the title of the third of the three highest Lamas, viz. of the Dharma Raja, residing in Bhotan, v. Cunningh. Ladak 371; Buddha Śakya-ṫúb-pa (Śakya thub pa) seems to have the same title, Pth. — c. fig.: bdé-ba-čan-gyi ʿjig-rtén (bde ba can gyi 'jig rten), or bdé-ʿgro mṫo-rís-kyi ʿjig-rtén (bde 'gro mtho ris kyi 'jig rten) the world of the blessed, like our 'heaven', but of rare occurrence. — 2. world, in a spiritual sense, ʿjig-rtén-gyi byá-ba ('jig rten gyi bya ba) worldly things or affairs; ʿjig-rtén-la dgós-pa ('jig rten la dgos pa) (or ṗán-pai (phan pa'i)) bsláb-bya (bslab bya) useful maxims of life, moral rules Glr.; ʿjig-rtén-gyi čos brgyad ('jig rten gyi chos brgyad), v. čos (chos); ʿjig-rtén byéd-pa ('jig rten byed pa) short expression for ʿjig-rtén-gyi las byéd-pa ('jig rten gyi las byed pa) Mil. — 3. symb. num.: three. — ʿjig-rtén-pa ('jig rten pa) 1. an inhabitant of the world, or the inhabitants of the world, the world as the totality of men, and more particularly of the worldly-minded; ʿjig-rtén-pa ni ma-dúl-ba yín-pas ('jig rten pa ni ma dul ba yin pas) as the world is unconverted, in which sense also ʿjig-rtén ('jig rten) (by itself) seems to be used. 2. a layman.

vb. 1. act. pf. bžig (bzhig), fut. gžig (gzhig), imp. (b)šig ((b)shig), W. *šig-de, šig taṅ-če* (*shig de, shig tang ce*), to destroy, buildings etc., frq.: to cut to pieces, to divide, e.g. a killed animal W.; to ruin, to annihilate, existing institutions or things, also other people; to abolish, annul, a law W.: to dissolve, an enchantment; to lay aside an assumed appearance or manner (= to unmask one's self) Mil.; to break, violate, one's duty, a vow, Dzl.;
